ege_demo2025_01.question_ru: Determine which of the listed elements have atoms in the ground state with the outer energy level electron configuration ns²np⁴.
ege_demo2025_01.explanation_ru: The configuration ns²np⁴ corresponds to group VIA (chalcogens). O: 2s²2p⁴, S: 3s²3p⁴. Cl has 3s²3p⁵ (group VII), N has 2s²2p³ (group V), Si has 3s²3p² (group IV).

ege_bank_01a.question_ru: Determine which of the listed elements have atoms in the ground state that contain two unpaired electrons.
ege_bank_01a.explanation_ru: C: 1s²2s²2p² — two unpaired electrons in 2p. O: 1s²2s²2p⁴ — two unpaired electrons in 2p. Be: 1s²2s² — no unpaired electrons. F: 1s²2s²2p⁵ — one unpaired electron. Ne: 1s²2s²2p⁶ — no unpaired electrons.

ege_demo2025_02.question_ru: From the listed chemical elements, select three that are in the same period of the Periodic Table. Arrange the selected elements in order of increasing electronegativity.
ege_demo2025_02.explanation_ru: Al (13), P (15), S (16) — all in the 3rd period. K (19) and Ca (20) are in the 4th period. Electronegativity increases from left to right: Al < P < S. Answer: 124.

ege_bank_02a.question_ru: From the listed chemical elements, select three that are in the same group. Arrange them in order of increasing atomic radius.
ege_bank_02a.explanation_ru: N (group V), P (group V), As (group V) — same group. O and S are in group VI. Atomic radius increases top to bottom: N < P < As. Answer: 124.

ege_demo2025_03.question_ru: From the listed elements, select two that exhibit a highest oxidation state of +3.
ege_demo2025_03.explanation_ru: Al (group III) — highest oxidation state +3. B (group III) — highest oxidation state +3. S has +6, C has +4, Na has +1.

ege_demo2025_04.question_ru: From the list below, select two substances that contain a covalent polar bond.
ege_demo2025_04.explanation_ru: NH₃ — covalent polar (N–H). H₂O — covalent polar (O–H). NaCl — ionic. O₂ — covalent nonpolar. Ca — metallic.

ege_bank_04a.question_ru: From the list below, select two substances with an ionic crystal lattice.
ege_bank_04a.explanation_ru: KBr and CaO are typical ionic compounds (metal + nonmetal). CO₂ and H₂S have molecular lattices. SiO₂ has an atomic (covalent network) lattice.

ege_demo2025_05.question_ru: Among the formulas/names of substances arranged in numbered cells, select the formulas/names of: 1) an acid; 2) an acidic oxide; 3) a base.
ege_demo2025_05.explanation_ru: Acid: H₂SO₄ (3) or HNO₃ (8). Acidic oxide: SO₃ (2) or CO₂ (7). Base: NaOH (1). One correct variant: A-3, B-2, C-1.

ege_demo2025_06.question_ru: From the list below, select two substances with which aluminum oxide reacts.
ege_demo2025_06.explanation_ru: Al₂O₃ is an amphoteric oxide. It reacts with bases (NaOH) and acids (HCl). With Na₂O — a refractory oxide, the reaction occurs only upon fusion. It does not react with H₂O. It does not react with NaCl.

ege_bank_06a.question_ru: From the list below, select two substances with which dilute sulfuric acid reacts.
ege_bank_06a.explanation_ru: Dilute H₂SO₄ reacts with metals above H in the activity series (Zn), and with salts of weak acids (Na₂CO₃). Cu and Ag are below H — they do not react with dilute H₂SO₄. SiO₂ does not react with dilute acids.

ege_demo2025_07.question_ru: Match each substance with the reagents with which it can react.
ege_demo2025_07.explanation_ru: S (simple substance) — reacts with Na, O₂, HNO₃ (3). SO₂ (acidic oxide) — with NaOH, CaO, H₂O (1). SO₃ (acidic oxide) — with KOH, BaCl₂ (BaSO₄↓), H₂O (5). Concentrated H₂SO₄ — with Cu, C, Fe (4).

ege_demo2025_10.question_ru: Match each reaction equation with the name of the substance that acts as the reducing agent.
ege_demo2025_10.explanation_ru: A: Fe is oxidized (0→+3) — reducing agent: iron (1). B: CO is oxidized (C: +2→+4) — reducing agent: carbon monoxide (2). C: no change in oxidation states — not a redox reaction, but conventionally HCl (5). D: Fe is oxidized (0→+2) — reducing agent: iron (1).

ege_demo2025_11.question_ru: Match each substance formula with the class/group to which it belongs.
ege_demo2025_11.explanation_ru: CH₃OH — methanol (monohydric alcohol, –OH group). CH₃CHO — acetaldehyde (–CHO group). CH₃COOH — acetic acid (–COOH group).

ege_bank_11a.question_ru: Match each substance name with the class/group of organic compounds.
ege_bank_11a.explanation_ru: Glycerol (C₃H₅(OH)₃) — polyhydric alcohol. Aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) — aromatic amine. Acetylene (C₂H₂) — alkyne.

ege_demo2025_12.question_ru: From the list below, select two substances that are isomers of butene-1.
ege_demo2025_12.explanation_ru: Butene-1 (C₄H₈). Isomers have the same molecular formula: butene-2 (C₄H₈) — positional isomer of the double bond; cyclobutane (C₄H₈) — inter-class isomer. Butane is C₄H₁₀, butadiene is C₄H₆, pentene is C₅H₁₀ — different formulas.

ege_demo2025_17.question_ru: From the list of reaction types, select two types that describe the reaction: 2Al + 6HCl = 2AlCl₃ + 3H₂↑
ege_demo2025_17.explanation_ru: Al replaces H in HCl → substitution reaction. Oxidation states change (Al: 0→+3, H: +1→0) → redox reaction. Irreversible (Al is more active than H). Not a double replacement (not two complex substances). Not a combination reaction (not one product from several).

ege_demo2025_18.question_ru: From the list below, select two external factors that lead to an increase in the rate of the reaction between sulfur and oxygen.
ege_demo2025_18.explanation_ru: Grinding sulfur increases the contact area → rate increases. The V₂O₅ catalyst accelerates the reaction. Lowering T slows it down. The concentration of SO₂ (product) does not affect the forward reaction. Pressure affects gases, but sulfur is a solid.

ege_demo2025_19.question_ru: Match each reaction equation with the shift in chemical equilibrium when pressure is increased.
ege_demo2025_19.explanation_ru: When pressure is increased, the equilibrium shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas. A: 3→2 (forward). B: 4→2 (forward). C: 2→2 (no effect). D: 1→2 (reverse).

ege_demo2025_20.question_ru: From the list below, select two substances that are strong electrolytes in aqueous solution.
ege_demo2025_20.explanation_ru: Strong electrolytes: NaOH (alkali), H₂SO₄ (strong acid). Weak electrolytes: H₂CO₃ (weak acid), CH₃COOH (weak acid), Cu(OH)₂ (insoluble base).

ege_demo2025_21.question_ru: Match each salt formula with the type of environment of its aqueous solution.
ege_demo2025_21.explanation_ru: Na₂CO₃ — salt of a strong base and a weak acid → alkaline environment (3). NaCl — salt of a strong base and a strong acid → neutral (2). FeCl₃ — salt of a weak base and a strong acid → acidic (1). K₂SO₄ — salt of a strong base and a strong acid → neutral (2).

ege_demo2025_27.question_ru: Calculate the mass fraction (in %) of salt in the solution obtained by mixing 200 g of a 5% solution and 300 g of a 15% solution of sodium chloride.
ege_demo2025_27.explanation_ru: m(NaCl)₁ = 200 × 0.05 = 10 g. m(NaCl)₂ = 300 × 0.15 = 45 g. Total mass of NaCl = 55 g. Total mass of solution = 500 g. ω = 55/500 × 100% = 11%.

ege_bank_27a.question_ru: Calculate the mass (in g) of the precipitate formed when 200 mL of a 0.1 M barium chloride solution is mixed with an excess of sodium sulfate solution.
ege_bank_27a.explanation_ru: n(BaCl₂) = C × V = 0.1 × 0.2 = 0.02 mol. BaCl₂ + Na₂SO₄ → BaSO₄↓ + 2NaCl. n(BaSO₄) = n(BaCl₂) = 0.02 mol. m(BaSO₄) = 0.02 × 233 = 4.66 ≈ 4.7 g.

ege_demo2025_28.question_ru: Calculate the volume (at STP, in liters) of gas released when an excess of hydrochloric acid acts on 21.2 g of sodium carbonate.
ege_demo2025_28.explanation_ru: Na₂CO₃ + 2HCl → 2NaCl + H₂O + CO₂↑. M(Na₂CO₃) = 106 g/mol. n(Na₂CO₃) = 21.2/106 = 0.2 mol. n(CO₂) = n(Na₂CO₃) = 0.2 mol. V(CO₂) = 0.2 × 22.4 = 4.48 ≈ 4.5 L.

ege_demo2025_29.question_ru:
Using the electron balance method, write the equation for the reaction:

KMnO₄ + HCl → KCl + MnCl₂ + Cl₂ + H₂O

Identify the oxidizing agent and the reducing agent.
ege_demo2025_29.explanation_ru:
Mn⁺⁷ + 5e⁻ → Mn²⁺ (reduction, KMnO₄ is the oxidizing agent)
2Cl⁻ − 2e⁻ → Cl₂⁰ (oxidation, HCl is the reducing agent)
Overall: Mn⁺⁷ |×2, Cl⁻ |×5 → 2KMnO₄ + 16HCl = 2KCl + 2MnCl₂ + 5Cl₂ + 8H₂O

ege_demo2025_30.question_ru:
Write the reaction equations to carry out the following transformations:

Al → Al₂O₃ → AlCl₃ → Al(OH)₃ → NaAlO₂ → Al₂(SO₄)₃
ege_demo2025_30.explanation_ru:
Aluminum transformation chain:
1) Burning Al in oxygen.
2) Dissolving Al₂O₃ in hydrochloric acid (amphoteric oxide + acid).
3) Precipitating Al(OH)₃ with a base (requires insufficient NaOH).
4) Dissolving Al(OH)₃ in excess NaOH (amphotericity).
5) Action of H₂SO₄ on sodium aluminate.

ege_demo2025_33.question_ru:
Write the reaction equations to carry out the transformations:

ethanol → ethylene → polyethylene
ethanol → acetaldehyde → acetic acid → ethyl acetate
ege_demo2025_33.explanation_ru:
1) Dehydration of alcohol (conc. H₂SO₄, >140 °C).
2) Polymerization of ethylene (Ziegler-Natta catalyst).
3) Oxidation of alcohol with CuO — producing aldehyde.
4) Oxidation of aldehyde to acid.
5) Esterification — producing an ester.

ege_demo2025_34.question_ru: When 9.0 g of an organic substance is burned, 17.6 g of CO₂ and 5.4 g of H₂O are produced. The vapor density relative to hydrogen is 45. Determine the molecular formula of the substance.
